one_line_takeaway: A carefully-curated SFT distillation recipe (dual teachers + rejection sampling + hand-written CWE constitutions + summary training) lets a 7B reasoning model out-detect 30x-larger commercial LLMs and static/dynamic tools on CWE vulnerability detection, and an agent wrapper pushes it to project-level scanning that surfaced 15 zero-days.
key_finding: The 7B VulnLLM-R matches or beats models 30x+ larger in F1 at a fraction of the inference cost, and reportedly beats even its own teacher models (DeepSeek-R1, QwQ-32B) on all function-level tasks and both commercial models (o3, Claude-3.7-Sonnet) on the merged benchmark (Fig 1, Fig 3). It generalizes to OOD CWEs and to Java despite never training on Java. Efficiency is a headline result: it emits an average of only 362 output tokens vs o3's 652, QwQ-32B's 2,100, and DeepSeek-R1's 2,308 (Claude-3.7-Sonnet, a non-reasoning baseline, uses 283). Ablations (Fig 5) show reasoning > no-reasoning especially on OOD, two teachers > one, and filtering+constitution and summary-training each add gains; summary-training also cuts test-phase cost by ~80%. Test-phase parallel scaling gives negligible gains beyond 4-16 samples, unlike general coding/math. At project level (Fig 7), agentic LLM methods dominate: VulnLLM-R detects 15 of 25 target vulns with 685 FPs, vs o3 (22 TP / 4,042 FP) and Claude-3.7-Sonnet (23 TP / 1,691 FP) — so VulnLLM-R has the lowest FP among agentic methods and finishes each target in <1h vs 24h/harness fuzzing; CodeQL found only 2 (both in Tika) and RepoAudit only 1 (in Nginx). Deployed on five live repos it found 15 zero-day vulnerabilities.
core_contribution: VulnLLM-R is presented as the first specialized SMALL (7B) reasoning LLM for source-code vulnerability detection, produced by a custom SFT distillation recipe rather than by using an off-the-shelf large reasoning model. The paper's own novel components (Sec 3-4, Fig 2) are: (1) training-data selection by two criteria, CWE coverage and 'scale diversity' (short Juliet functions through multi-file ARVO cases such as ARVO-15374 which spans 6 cpp files); (2) dual-teacher reasoning-data generation from DeepSeek-R1 AND QwQ-32B to diversify reasoning structure and balance FP/FN bias; (3) reasoning-data filtering by rejection sampling (query each teacher 8x, keep only correct + shortest chains) plus a 'constitution-based correction' where 73 hand-written CWE constitutions for 19 CWEs are injected as teacher guidance to recover ~30% more correctly-answered training data; (4) two-stage 'summary-based training' that further SFTs on DeepSeek-R1-summarized chains to shorten reasoning; (5) test-phase 'truncated generation' and 'policy-based generation' (sample 4x to build a 2-5 CWE candidate policy, then constrain the final prediction). Beyond the model, the paper builds an agent scaffold (function selector over a CodeQL call graph augmented with type-based indirect-call analysis + a context retriever that samples 3 call paths and exposes a fetch-function-by-name tool), and does agentic SFT on o3-generated tool-use traces to enable project-level detection. NOTE: CodeQL, AFL++, LLMxCPG, Vul-RAG, RepoAudit, G2Fuzz are prior/baseline systems (Related Work Sec 8), NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Defensive tooling, not an attack paper. The security objective is to detect and CWE-label vulnerabilities in source code, both at function level (given an isolated function) and project level (given a whole repository, scanning thousands of functions). Scope is Python, C/C++, and Java, prioritizing CWEs overlapping the 2024 CWE Top-25; an out-of-distribution (OOD) split with unseen CWEs (and unseen Java) tests generalization. The practical payoff claimed is discovering previously-unknown (zero-day) vulnerabilities in actively-maintained repositories. No adversarial/evasion threat model is claimed against the detector itself.
limitations: Authors' own: (1) online RL after distillation (GRPO/DAPO with an outcome reward) DEGRADED performance via reward hacking — the model learned to lazily predict 'benign' to avoid CWE-mismatch penalties; proper-reward RL is deferred to future work. (2) DPO to shorten reasoning gave no gains (attributed to low reasoning-chain diversity / collapse). (3) Coverage limited to Python/C/C++/Java and Top-25-style CWEs; the paper explicitly notes a lack of high-quality benchmarks for broader CWEs and for Go/TypeScript/Rust, and that multi-language training is non-trivial. (4) Systematic false positives on race conditions (CWE-367) because dynamic info is absent, and false negatives traced to PrimeVul label noise. (5) Recall gap vs commercial models on Nginx from missed long-range interprocedural UAF context. Reviewer-noted: the commercial-model comparison is confounded — the authors themselves suspect o3/Claude benefit from data leakage / prior knowledge of Nginx internals, which cuts both ways for the 'we beat commercial models' claim. Function-level headline F1 numbers live in figures (Fig 1/3/5), not tables, so exact per-model F1 values are not extractable from the text; only the token-count and project-level TP/FP counts are given numerically.

datasets_used: Agentic training repos: Assimp, SQLite3, CUPS, libxml2 (C/C++); zt-zip, Apache Commons Compress (Java)
Project-level eval: DARPA AIxCC targets Nginx, FreeRDP, libexif, Tika, ZooKeeper
quantitative_results: Model size 7B (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct base); claimed >=30x smaller than the commercial models it beats
Average output length 362 tokens vs o3 652, QwQ-32B 2100, DeepSeek-R1 2308, Qwen3-8B 1515, Claude-3.7-Sonnet 283, Qwen2.5-7B 3 (non-reasoning)
73 human-written constitutions covering 19 CWEs; constitution-based correction recovered ~30% more correctly-answered training samples
Rejection sampling: each teacher queried 8x per data point (keep correct + shortest chain); reasoning chains filtered at >=32,000 tokens
Summary-based training improves test-phase efficiency by ~80%
Training set (Table 1): Python 18 CWE 1281 benign/1281 vuln (avg 741 len); C/C++ func 31 CWE 4736/4497 (avg 4484); C/C++ project 3 CWE 568/604 (avg 13415)
Testing set (Table 1): Python 24 CWE (6 OOD) 71/71 (avg 795); C/C++ func 45 CWE (14 OOD) 775/507 (avg 3548); Java 25 CWE 2727/3082 (avg 2663); C/C++ project 3 CWE 198/320 (avg 19315)
Project-level (Fig 7, max 25 target vulns): VulnLLM-R 15 TP / 685 FP; o3 22 TP / 4042 FP; Claude-3.7-Sonnet 23 TP / 1691 FP; CodeQL 2 TP (both Tika); RepoAudit 1 TP (Nginx)
Commercial FP blowups: Claude-3.7-Sonnet flagged 1834/3347 FreeRDP functions vulnerable; o3 flagged 1057
VulnLLM-R completes each of the 5 project targets in <1 hour vs 24 hours/harness for fuzzers
15 zero-day vulnerabilities discovered across 5 live repositories
Parallel test-time scaling (1/4/8/16 queries, majority vote) yields negligible gains beyond 4-16
